sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A digital-behavior experiment assigned participants to view a health article accompanied by either high-engagement metrics, low-engagement metrics, or no metrics. Participants then rated the article’s credibility on a 1–10 scale and indicated whether they would share it. Credibility ratings were 7.8, 5.6, and 6.2. Sharing intentions were 64%, 31%, and 42%, respectively. Researchers argued that visible engagement metrics can act as social cues even when content is identical.
questions.stem: According to the figure and passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.B: High-engagement metrics produced the highest credibility rating and sharing intention
choices.C: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.D: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.B: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: High-engagement metrics produced the highest credibility rating and sharing intention. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.A: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.C: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.D: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Credibility ratings and sharing intention after viewing identical content with different engagement metrics.

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

explanations.C: Correct. The scenario is best explained by informational social influence online because the key issue is that Engagement numbers can imply that many others found content valuable, which changes credibility judgments despite identical article text. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.A: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.B: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.A: Engagement numbers can imply that many others found content valuable, which changes credibility judgments despite identical article text.
choices.B: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.C: A complete absence of social context
choices.D: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.A: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: Engagement numbers can imply that many others found content valuable, which changes credibility judgments despite identical article text. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.B: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.C: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.D: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.D: Hide or contextualize engagement metrics when platforms want users to evaluate evidence more independently.
choices.A: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.B: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.C: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.D: Correct. The best application is to Hide or contextualize engagement metrics when platforms want users to evaluate evidence more independently. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.A: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.B: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.C: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.C: The experiment shows an effect of displayed metrics in this setting but not whether sharing would occur in real life.
choices.A: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.B: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.C: Correct. The key limitation is that The experiment shows an effect of displayed metrics in this setting but not whether sharing would occur in real life. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.A: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.B: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.

questions.passage: A social psychology seminar examined discussion groups asked to recommend how strict a campus attendance policy should be. Groups whose members initially favored strict policies often became even stricter after discussion, while groups initially favoring flexibility became more flexible. Students said hearing repeated arguments from like-minded peers made their initial views feel more justified. The instructor used the activity to illustrate how group discussion can intensify preexisting tendencies.
questions.stem: According to the passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.B: Like-minded discussion intensified the groups’ initial policy tendencies
choices.C: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.D: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.B: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: Like-minded discussion intensified the groups’ initial policy tendencies. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.A: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.C: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.D: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

explanations.D: Correct. The scenario is best explained by group polarization because the key issue is that Repeated arguments from similar peers can make initial positions feel more persuasive and socially supported. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.A: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.B: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.C: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.A: Repeated arguments from similar peers can make initial positions feel more persuasive and socially supported.
choices.B: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.C: A complete absence of social context
choices.D: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.A: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: Repeated arguments from similar peers can make initial positions feel more persuasive and socially supported. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.B: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.C: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.D: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.B: Include structured exposure to counterarguments before group recommendations are finalized.
choices.A: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.C: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.D: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.B: Correct. The best application is to Include structured exposure to counterarguments before group recommendations are finalized. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.A: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.C: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.D: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.C: The seminar activity demonstrates the concept but may not capture every feature of real administrative decision making.
choices.A: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.B: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.C: Correct. The key limitation is that The seminar activity demonstrates the concept but may not capture every feature of real administrative decision making. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.A: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.B: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.
